prompt: What is the boiling point of water at sea level, and how does it change on the surface of Mars?
text:
The boiling point of water at sea level is 100 degrees Celsius (212 degrees Fahrenheit) under standard atmospheric pressure (1 atmosphere or 101.3 kPa). 

On the surface of Mars, the atmospheric pressure is significantly lower, averaging about 0.6 kPa (less than 1% of Earth's atmospheric pressure). Because of this reduced pressure, water would boil at a much lower temperature. Estimates suggest that the boiling point of water on Mars could be around 0 degrees Celsius (32 degrees Fahrenheit) or even lower, depending on the exact pressure conditions. This means that water can exist as a liquid only under very specific conditions on Mars, often requiring the presence of salts or other factors to increase its boiling point and stabilize it in liquid form.
